question: Text someone in this room's contact list 'your name came up tonight' and show everyone the reply.
Send a voice note to your last three contacts saying 'I've been thinking about you.' No explanation.
Let the group write a caption for your most recent Instagram photo and post it for one hour.
Show everyone your camera roll from exactly one year ago. No deleting before you hand the phone over.
Read the last voice note you sent out loud in a dramatically different accent. Everyone listens.
Stand up and give a 30-second best man or maid of honor speech about the person to your right.
Write a two-sentence apology to someone you've wronged and read it to the group. You don't have to say who it's for.
Search your name in your own messages and read the first result you find out loud.
Let your partner read the last five things you searched on your phone and react in real time.
Tell the group something you've been putting off confronting. Then say one concrete step you'll actually take.
Walk up to a mirror, look yourself in the eye, and say one thing you actually need to hear right now.
Go through your most-used emojis and explain the story behind each of the top five. No skipping.
Pick someone in the room and roast them in exactly three sentences. They're not allowed to respond for sixty seconds.
Narrate everything the person across from you does for the next two minutes like a nature documentary. Do not break character.
Draw a quick portrait of how you see yourself right now — not how you look, how you feel. Show it and explain one detail.
Assign everyone in the room a job based purely on their personality. Say the job and the reason out loud. No softening it.
Compliment every person in the room in a way that sounds like an insult. They decide if it lands as sweet or savage.
Pitch yourself as a contestant on a reality dating show. Group asks you three casting questions. No hesitation allowed.
Direct your partner in a 30-second silent film scene from your relationship. They act. You shout stage directions.
Debate, with full conviction, why a completely mundane object is the greatest invention in human history. One minute. Go.
Conduct a fake job interview with the person to your left. You're hiring for a role that exposes their biggest flaw. Keep it professional.
Narrate a fake movie trailer for a film about the most embarrassing thing that's ever happened to you. Title it. Cast it.
Stand up and deliver a one-minute TED Talk about the single belief you hold that most people in this room would disagree with.
Charades — but you can only use facial expressions. No body movement. Group has 60 seconds to guess the movie you're given.
Negotiate with your partner for one real-life privilege — a free pass, a favor, anything. Make your case out loud. Group decides if you deserve it.
Improvise a 60-second cooking show segment using only items visible in this room as fake ingredients. Name the dish.
Do your best impression of how each person in this room would handle being caught in a lie. Act each one out. No names — let them guess.
Recreate your first argument as a full dramatic scene — you play your partner, they play you. Group judges who's more accurate.
Host a 90-second fake game show where you quiz the group on made-up rules to a sport you invent on the spot right now.
Give a eulogy for a personality trait you used to have that you've outgrown. Be sincere. Group can only applaud at the end.
Draw a Venn diagram on paper showing what you and the person across from you have in common versus where you completely differ. Present it.
Stand up. For 60 seconds, speak only in compliments — directed at yourself. Group is not allowed to laugh or react until you're done.
Announce to the group your most embarrassing talent. Then perform it. No miming — actual performance.
Guess what everyone in this room had for breakfast today. Say each guess out loud with full confidence.
Re-enact how you think your partner acts when you're not around. Thirty seconds. They score your accuracy.
Confess a small, petty thing you've been silently judging someone in this room for. One sentence. Own it.
Describe the version of yourself you were five years ago as if introducing a stranger to the group. Be honest.
Using only sound effects — no words — act out the last time you cried. Group guesses what happened.
Pick one person in the room and pitch a conspiracy theory about their daily routine. Make it convincing. They cannot deny anything.
Recreate your first impression of your partner — the exact vibe, posture, expression. They guess what you were actually thinking.
Stand up and deliver a formal acceptance speech for an award the group invents for you right now based on your personality.
Invent a word that doesn't exist, define it live, use it in three sentences. Group votes whether it should be real.
Mime the emotion you feel most often but never talk about. Hold the expression for thirty seconds. No explaining after.
Improvise a 60-second courtroom closing argument defending the most questionable decision you made this year.
Without using any names, describe everyone in this room as a movie genre. Say the genre and one sentence explaining why.
Teach your partner something — anything — in exactly 60 seconds as if they know absolutely nothing. Group scores your teaching.
Pick any two people in the room and argue, with evidence, that they are secretly the same person. One minute. No backing down.
Name the person in this room you've underestimated. Tell them one specific thing you now see that you missed before.
Freestyle rap for 30 seconds about the person to your right using only facts you actually know about them. No compliments allowed.
Do a live weather forecast for the emotional climate of this room right now. Point at people. Use technical terms.
Propose a fake constitutional amendment that would fix your biggest relationship pet peeve. Read it formally. Your partner may not object.
Stand up. Without naming anyone, act out the moment in your life you most wish you'd had a witness for. No explanation after.
You have 60 seconds to convince the group that a completely random number — chosen right now by the group — is secretly meaningful. Go.
Translate the last awkward silence in this room into a full 30-second dramatic scene. Act both parts. Give it a title.
Invent a board game on the spot. Name it, explain the rules in 30 seconds, and demonstrate one turn using people in this room.
Deliver a 45-second graduation speech to your past self. Pick the exact age. Group cannot laugh until you finish.
Choreograph and perform a 30-second interpretive dance representing the group dynamic tonight. No music needed. Full commitment required.
Argue — with complete conviction — that your worst habit is actually your most attractive quality. Partner is the judge.
Using only your body, spell out a word chosen secretly by the group. No clues. You have 45 seconds. Group guesses.
Pick the person you know least well here. Describe their life story as you imagine it — confidently, with detail. They score your accuracy.
Describe the version of yourself you're most afraid other people already see. One minute. No softening it.
Invent a signature cocktail named after your own personality. Describe every ingredient and why it belongs. Group rates the concept.
Lip sync a song chosen by the group with zero warning. Full performance — movement, expression, commitment. Group scores you out of ten.
Everyone writes down one word. You have 90 seconds to weave all of them into a single coherent bedtime story. Tell it out loud.
You are a news anchor. Deliver a 60-second breaking news segment about something trivial that actually happened in this room tonight.
Stand up and reenact — in real time — how you physically behave when you have a crush on someone. No narrating. Just show it.
Pick two people in the room and officiate a fake 45-second wedding between their worst personality traits. Name the traits. Marry them properly.
Write your own two-sentence obituary right now. Read it aloud. Group is not allowed to comment for fifteen seconds after.
Describe your relationship as a genre of film you'd never watch voluntarily. Name the film, the rating, and the one-line synopsis. Your partner reacts.
Choose a person in the room. Draw their portrait using only your non-dominant hand. Sixty seconds. Show everyone without apologizing for it.
Stand in front of the group and pitch yourself as a brand. Name, tagline, target market, one weakness. Full deck. Thirty seconds. Go.
Everyone secretly votes on an animal that represents your personality. You must guess what animal you got before they reveal it.
Recite the last compliment someone gave you that you didn't believe. Say it again — this time like you actually do.
Improvise a 60-second infomercial for the most annoying habit of the person to your left. Sell it as a lifestyle product. Name it.
The group gives you a celebrity, an accent, and a topic. You have 45 seconds to deliver a monologue combining all three. No warm-up.
Make sustained eye contact with one person and, without smiling, slowly explain why you find them intimidating. They cannot look away.
Walk around the room and silently assign everyone a superhero power based on their biggest flaw. Announce all of them at once at the end.
Tell the group a story about yourself using three true sentences and one lie. Everyone guesses which is false. You reveal nothing until all votes are in.
Using only your voice — no words, just sounds — perform the opening scene of a horror movie. Group votes thumbs up or down after thirty seconds.
You have ninety seconds to write a personal ad for yourself — flaws listed first, strengths last. Read it exactly as written. No edits allowed.
Group secretly picks a famous villain. You must defend that villain's choices for sixty seconds with complete moral conviction. Group cross-examines you.
Pick any object in this room. You have 45 seconds to teach the group a fake martial art based on it. Name the style. Demonstrate three moves.
Stand up. Point to someone. Say one thing you've never said to them out loud — but only if you mean it completely. No softening allowed.
Without speaking, use only props in this room to build a physical monument to your relationship's biggest inside joke. Sixty seconds. Partner guesses what it is.
Pick one person. Guess their most-used phrase, their resting face, and their walk — then demonstrate all three back-to-back. They score each one.
Stand up. Using only furniture and people as props, build a physical timeline of the biggest decision you've ever made. Walk the group through it.
Cold-pitch a start-up to the group in sixty seconds. The product must solve a problem that only exists in this specific friend group. Take questions.
Without coaching your partner, mime a scene from your first date. They narrate what they think is happening in real time.
Face the room and recite every compliment you've ever received that you genuinely believed. Keep going until you run out.
Guess the most embarrassing thing each person here has Googled in the last week. One guess per person. Say them all at once.
Write one sentence on paper that you've never said to anyone. Fold it. Group decides — you read it aloud or burn it unread.
Dub a silent 30-second scene that the group acts out in real time. You provide all voices and sound effects. No script. Go.
Sell an imaginary product to the group using only mime. No words, no sounds. Group has 45 seconds to guess what you're selling.
Stand up. Shake hands with every person in the room and say one word — only one — that you think defines them. No repeats.
Announce a fake recall of a household product. Deliver the safety warning with full government-official energy. Group asks follow-up questions.
Walk to the door. Pretend you're leaving this friendship group for good. Give a ten-second goodbye. Then sit back down and say nothing.
Invent a handshake-based secret society on the spot. Recruit one member, teach the ritual, name the society. Group decides if they'd join.
Translate your relationship into a two-sentence IKEA instruction manual. Read it aloud. Your partner rates assembly difficulty out of five.
Stand up and present a thirty-second argument for why you specifically should survive a zombie apocalypse. Group votes immediately after. No rebuttals.
Auction off an invisible object to the room. Name it, set a starting bid, and hype it for 45 seconds. Highest bidder must act thrilled.
